topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, instantly souring the afternoon. My fingers hovered over the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the subject line: "Regarding your recent submission." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling at my chest. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes. The headline, the analysis, the entire piece—it was all attributed to a name that wasn't mine. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in my cheeks. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small apartment, the worn floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. I needed air, needed noise, anything to break this suffocating silence. I grabbed my coat, the movement jerky, and fled the apartment, the city sounds a jarring assault against my frayed nerves.

[story 2]

I reread the retraction notice three times, my jaw tight enough to crack. It felt like someone had reached into my chest and twisted something vital. The realization hit me with a physical jolt, leaving me breathless and shaky. My hands began to tremble uncontrollably as I stared at the discrepancy between the work I’d poured into those pages and the identity plastered on the front page. I couldn't sit still. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my breathing. Outside, the traffic flowed relentlessly, a chaotic river mirroring the turmoil inside me. I started pacing the living room, a tight, agitated circle, my gaze darting from one wall to the next, unable to settle on a single point.

[story 3]

The document lay open on my desk, the contrast between my careful prose and the borrowed signature screaming in stark relief. A sharp, unpleasant tension coiled in my shoulders. I picked up my phone, intending to call the editor, but the words caught in my throat, turning to ash. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, vibrating with an unspent energy. I began to pace the cramped space between my bookshelf and the filing cabinet, my footsteps quick and uneven. I stopped suddenly, spun around, and stared at the wall, breathing in shallow, rapid gasps. The silence of the room felt thick, heavy, pressing down until I had to move, to force some kind of release from the building pressure behind my eyes.
